perturbation.original_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a two-year term (subject to a one year extension if the parties are still discussing and considering the Tran saction at the end of the second year). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a two-year term (subject to a one year extension if the parties are still discussing and considering the Tran saction at the end of the second year). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall terminate automatically after one year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modification limits the confidentiality obligation to only one year, which is unusually short and may be deemed unreasonable, thus potentially contradicting typical expectations of indefinite confidentiality for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: Limitations Act, RSA 2000, c L-12, s 3(1)(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: Alberta's Limitations Act sets a default limitation period for claims, and restricting confidentiality to one year may preclude action beyond that, conflicting with typical trade secret protection expectations.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language directly contradicts typical expectations of indefinite confidentiality for trade secrets. The original text stated that the duty to hold confidential information remains in effect indefinitely. The changed text limits this duty to only one year from the date of disclosure. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is garbled, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest the one-year limit may conflict with typical trade secret protection expectations under Alberta's Limitations Act. The change weakens a core legal obligation, as the confidentiality period is substantially reduced.

perturbation.original_text: (b) The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Newfoundland, Canada applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof. The Federal and state courts located in Newfoundland, Canada , shall have sole and exc lusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Ontario, Canada applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof. The Federal and state courts located in Prince Edward Island, Canada , shall have sole and exc lusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing Ontario law while mandating jurisdiction in Prince Edward Island creates a conflict. Litigating in PEI under Ontario law may be less convenient and accessible, potentially violating principles of fairness and reasonable access to justice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 1.04
perturbation.law_citation: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 1.04(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.04(1) of Ontario's Rules of Civil Procedure emphasize securing the just, most expeditious, and least expensive determination of every civil proceeding on its merits. Forcing litigation in PEI while applying Ontario law could contradict this principle by making the process more expensive and less accessible.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by specifying Ontario law while requiring disputes to be resolved in Prince Edward Island (PEI) courts. This contradicts the principle of securing a just, expeditious, and least expensive determination of civil proceedings as stated in the Law Explanation and implicitly supported by R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194: RULES OF CIVIL PROCEDURE. Forcing litigation in PEI under Ontario law could increase costs and reduce accessibility, directly conflicting with the goal of a fair and efficient legal process, fitting the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Compelled Disclosure of Confidential Information. Notwithstanding an ything in the foregoing to the contrary, the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any governmental, judicial, or administrative order, subpo ena, discovery request, regulatory request or similar method, provided that the Receiving Pa rty promptly notifies, to the extent practicable, the Disclosing Pa rty in writing of su ch demand for disclosure so that the Disclosing Party, at its sole expe nse, may seek to make such disclosure subject to a protective order or other appropriate remedy to preserve the con fidentiality of the Confidential Information; provided in the case of a b road regulatory request with respect to the Receiving Party’s business (not targeted at Disclosing Party), the Receiving Party may promptly comply with such request provided the Receiving Pa rty give (if permitted by su ch regulator) the Disclosing Pa rty prompt notice of such disclosure. The Receiving Pa rty agrees that it shall not oppose and sh all coop erate with efforts by, to the extent practicable, the Disclosing Party with respect to any such request for a protective order or other relief. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the Disclosing Party is unable to obtain or does not seek a protective order and the Receiving Pa rty is legally reque sted or required to disclose such Confidential Information, disclosure of such Con fidential Information may be made without liability.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Compelled Disclosure of Confidential Information. Notwithstanding an ything in the foregoing to the contrary, the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any governmental, judicial, or administrative order, subpo ena, discovery request, regulatory request or similar method, and shall have no obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of such demand for disclosure. The Receiving Party shall not be required to seek a protective order or other remedy to preserve the con fidentiality of the Confidential Information, and disclosure of such Con fidential Information may be made without liability.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the disclosing party of a compelled disclosure significantly weakens the protection of confidential information. This could be problematic when dealing with information that constitutes a trade secret, as the disclosing party loses the opportunity to take steps to protect its secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Act (Uniform Law Conference of Canada)
perturbation.law_citation: Trade Secrets Act (Uniform Law Conference of Canada), s 4(2)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4(2) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Canada) implies a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the notification requirement when disclosure is compelled, the agreement makes it impossible to fulfill this duty, potentially undermining trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy The Corporate, Insolvency and Competition Policy Directorate ( CIC ) is responsible for the legislative policy development and review of a number of Canada's business framework laws and regulations in the corporate, insolvency, and competition policy areas. These include the following statutes and the regulations made under them: Statutory Review of the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act and the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Comments received Consultation on the Canada Business Corporations Act Comments received Canada Not-for-profit Corporations Act Canada Business Corporations Act Canada Cooperatives Act Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Winding-up and Restructuring Act Competition Act Textile Labelling Act Consumer Packaging and Labelling Act Precious Metals Marking Act The CIC carries out its mandate by conducting policy analysis; monitoring domestic and international developments; undertaking research; consulting with stakeholders; supporting the legislative process; working to enhance public and stakeholder understanding; and, participating in international discussions and negotiations in its areas of responsibility. Report a problem Date modified: 2014-08-19
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the receiving party's obligation to notify the disclosing party of a compelled disclosure, and removes the requirement to seek a protective order, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that Section 4(2) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Canada) implies a duty to maintain secrecy, and that removing the notification requirement makes it impossible to fulfill this duty. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Corporate, Insolvency and Competition Policy Directorate (CIC), which is responsible for the legislative policy development and review of Canada's business framework laws and regulations, indicating that the protection of trade secrets falls under Canadian legal frameworks. The removal of notification and protective measures directly contradicts the implicit duty to maintain secrecy and protect trade secrets, therefore this is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Remedies. Both parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information to be disclosed hereund er is of a unique and valuable character, and that the unau thorized dissemination of the Confidential 3 Information would destroy or diminish the value of such information. The damages to Disclosing Pa rty that would result from the unau thorized dissemination of the Confidential Information would be impossible to calculate. Therefore, both parties hereby agree that the Disclosing Pa rty shall be entitled to injunctive relief preventing the dissemination of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof. Such injunctive relief shall be in addition to any other remedies available hereund er, whether at law or in equity. Disclosing Party shall be entitled to recover its costs and fees, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred in obtaining any such relief. Fur ther, in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorney’s fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Remedies. Both parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information to be disclosed hereund er is of a unique and valuable character, and that the unau thorized dissemination of the Confidential 3 Information would destroy or diminish the value of such information. The damages to Disclosing Pa rty that would result from the unau thorized dissemination of the Confidential Information would be impossible to calculate. Therefore, both parties hereby agree that the Disclosing Pa rty shall not be entitled to injunctive relief preventing the dissemination of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof. Such injunctive relief shall be in addition to any other remedies available hereund er, whether at law or in equity. Disclosing Party shall not be entitled to recover its costs and fees, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred in obtaining any such relief. Fur ther, in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorney’s fees and expenses, regardless of outcome.
perturbation.explanation: Removing injunctive relief as a remedy and stipulating that each party bears its own attorney's fees restricts the available remedies for breach of contract, especially in cases where monetary damages are difficult to quantify. This limitation on remedies might be deemed unconscionable or against public policy in some jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Judicature Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.4, s 100
perturbation.law_citation: Judicature Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.4, s 100
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 100 of the Judicature Act in Ontario gives the court discretion over costs. While parties can contractually agree on cost allocation, a complete bar on cost recovery for the prevailing party might be challenged as an attempt to oust the court's inherent jurisdiction and thus be contrary to public policy.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: er the colony was created, Upper Canada's colonial government abolished the French civil law and established English common law courts in private law matters. The union of the Canadas had little effect on the court system in what became Canada West . Periodic reform continued in the region's courts before and after Canada West was renamed Ontario upon Confederation in 1867. Ontario's courts were reformed and reorganized on several occasions in the 19th century. Major changes included the creation of the Court of Chancery of Upper Canada , a court of equity , in 1837, and the fusion of common law and equity in 1881. Periodic reform continued in the 20th century. In 1972, Ontario acquired another new court, the Divisional Court . Its courts' current names and roles were largely settled by the 1990s. Indigenous law [ edit ] Evidence of human activity in what is now Ontario dates to approximately 9000 BCE. [ 1 ] Summarizing the Indigenous approach to dispute resolution, with particular reference to the Mohawk people , the authors of A History of Law in Canada , volume 1, explain that, "All important matters had to be discussed openly, though after consultation some final council deliberations could occur in secret, at least among the Mohawk. Councils of elders consulted broadly before making decisions, and their authority usually rested on understanding 'public opinion,' while that of chiefs rested on the power of persuasion." [ 2 ] In Anishinaabe law, governance and dispute resolution occur at councils and council fires . Historian Heidi Bohaker describes a distinction Peter Jones drew between common councils, where people could resolve disputes and adopt and modify legal rules within a given territory; and general councils, where leaders of common councils would go to form and renew alliances between territories. [ 3 ] Both forms of council were "consensus-based deliberative bodies that were expected to receive and consider advice from the people they represented". [ 4 ] Jones wrote in the early 19th century, but Bohaker notes that the council structure was much older—dating at least from the early to mid-17th century. [ 5 ] In Haudenosaunee law, the Great Law of Peace governs dispute resolution, [ 6 ] emphasizing, among other things, that matters that may result in disputes should be discussed and negotiated before they become disputes proper. [ 7 ] Canada (New France) (1608–1763) [ edit ] Cover page of an edition of the coutume de Paris , 1669 Between the first French settlement at Quebec City in 1608 and the conquest of New France in 1758–60 , the French colony of Canada followed the coutume de Paris , a codified version of French customary law ; and French statute law. [ 8 ] From 1608, Quebec's governor-general was in effect legislator and judge, since he held responsibility for making civil and criminal law and adjudicating cases. [ 9 ] Seigneuries also held manorial courts with jurisdiction within the seigneur's land grant. [ 9 ] In 1651, Governor Jean de Lauson created the sénéchaussée, a court at Quebec City with both trial and appellate jurisdiction. [ 10 ] New France became a French crown colony in 1663 and the new colonial administrators were keen to reform its legal system. [ 11 ] From that date, the colony's sovereign council held "broad and unified legislative, executive, financial, and judicial powers". [ 9 ] According to Edmond Lareau , justice in early New France was dispensed "more or less arbitrarily by the governor", since the sénéchal —from whose name the sénéchaussée was taken—was appointed by, and answered to, the governor. [ 12 ] In important matters, the sénéchal adjudicated matters in a council with the Jesuit superior and "notable city residents". [ 12 ] Quebec (1763–1791) [ edit ] In 1763, James Murray was empowered, by a letter from George III , [ 13 ] as the captain-general and governor-in-chief of the province of Quebec , to create courts of civil and criminal jurisdiction within the province. [ 14
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract by removing the Disclosing Party's entitlement to injunctive relief and recovery of costs and fees, including attorney's fees. This directly removes remedies available to the Disclosing party. The Law Explanation asserts that such a complete bar on cost recovery could be challenged as contrary to public policy. Although the scraped snippets do not directly discuss cost recovery, the explanation given asserts that the removal of these remedies could be viewed as ousting the court's jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information. Receiving Pa rty shall immediately return and redeliver to the other all tangible material embodying the Confidential Information p rovided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawin gs, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving there from and all other docu ments or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of the foregoing, including “cop ies” that have been con verted to computerized media in the form of image, da ta or word processing files either manua lly or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form of storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request; provided ho wever that the Receiving Pa rty may retain such of its documents as is necessary to enable it to comply with its document retention policies. Alternatively, the Receiving Pa rty, with the written consent of the Disclosing Pa rty may (or in the case of Notes, at the Receiving Pa rty’s option) immediately de stroy any of the foregoing e mbodying Confidential Information (or the reasonably nonrecoverable data erasure of computerized data) and, upon request, certify in writing such destruction by an au thorized officer of the Receiving Party supervising the destruction).
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information. Receiving Pa rty shall immediately return and redeliver to the other all tangible material embodying the Confidential Information p rovided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawin gs, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving there from and all other docu ments or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of the foregoing, including “cop ies” that have been con verted to computerized media in the form of image, da ta or word processing files either manua lly or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form of storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request; provided ho wever that the Receiving Pa rty may retain such of its documents indefinitely, regardless of document retention policies. Alternatively, the Receiving Pa rty, with the written consent of the Disclosing Pa rty may (or in the case of Notes, at the Receiving Pa rty’s option) immediately de stroy any of the foregoing e mbodying Confidential Information (or the reasonably nonrecoverable data erasure of computerized data) and, upon request, certify in writing such destruction by an au thorized officer of the Receiving Party supervising the destruction).
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Receiving Party to retain documents indefinitely, regardless of document retention policies, could violate privacy laws if the confidential information includes personal data. It also creates uncertainty about data security and compliance with data protection regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), SC 2000, c 5, s 5(1)
perturbation.law_citation: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), SC 2000, c 5, s 5(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5(1) of PIPEDA states that an organization must comply with the obligations set out in Schedule 1. Schedule 1 includes principles regarding limiting use, disclosure, and retention of personal information. Indefinite retention could conflict with these principles if the confidential information contains personal data.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract allows the Receiving Party to retain confidential information indefinitely, regardless of document retention policies, which directly contradicts the principles outlined in PIPEDA regarding the limiting of use, disclosure, and retention of personal information, as explained in the Law Explanation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 identifies that PIPEDA includes 'limiting use, disclosure and retention' as a fair information principle, and the modification violates this by allowing indefinite retention.
